rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion In Support Of Uber Technologies, Inc.S Petition To Quash Tax Collectors Subpoena For Driver Information (Discovery)
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON THURSDAY, JUNE 22, 2017, LINE 3 PETITIONER UBER TECHNOLOGIE INC.'S Petition To Quash Tax Collectors Subpoena For Driver Information DENIED. Respondent City and County of San Francisco Office of the Treasurer-Tax Collector has shown that it is authorized by section 6.4-1 of the San Francisco Business and Tax Regulations Code to issue the administrative subpoena, the subpoena serves a valid purpose of investigating compliance with section 6.2-12 of the Code, and the information subpoenaed is pertinent to such a determination. Uber's quasi-constitutional challenges to the subpoena based on vagueness of the regulatory scheme and the prohibition of extraterritorial application of local ordinances are premature in light of Western Oil & Gas Assn. v. State Board of Equalization (1987) 44 Cal.3d 208, 214. Western Oil directs the court to only examine the Tax Collector's authority to issue the subpoena and not to determine the ultimate validity of the tax assessment. The subpoena is not overly broad and Uber has not shown that compliance would be unduly burdensome. Uber also failed to show that the driver information requested constitutes a protected trade secret or that disclosure would violate drivers' privacy or due process rights. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the respondent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/RBU

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of San Franciscos Motion For An Order To Require Uber Technologies, Inc. To Disclose Driver Contact Information To The San Francisco Tax Collector
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON THURSDAY, JUNE 22, 2017, LINE 4 PETITIONERS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, SAN FRANCISCO TREASURER JOSE CISNEROS and SAN FRANCISCO TAX COLLECTOR DAVID AUGUSTINE'S Motion For An Order To Require Uber Technologies, Inc. To Disclose Driver Contact Information To The San Francisco Tax Collector GRANTED. Petitioners have shown that the Tax Collector is authorized by section 6.4-1 of the San Francisco Business and Tax Regulations Code to issue the administrative subpoena, the subpoena serves a valid purpose of investigating compliance with section 6.2-12 of the Code, and the information subpoenaed is pertinent to such a determination. Uber's quasi-constitutional challenges to the subpoena based on vagueness of the regulatory scheme and the prohibition of extraterritorial application of local ordinances are premature in light of Western Oil & Gas Assn. v. State Board of Equalization (1987) 44 Cal.3d 208, 214. Western Oil directs the court to only examine the Tax Collector's authority to issue the subpoena and not to determine the ultimate validity of the tax assessment. The subpoena is not overly broad and Uber has not shown that compliance would be unduly burdensome. Uber also failed to show that the driver information requested constitutes a protected trade secret or that disclosure would violate drivers' privacy or due process rights. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for petitioners is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/RBU

rulings.Case Title: ANTHONY R ORNANO VS. INSIKT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Special Interrogatories And Demand For Production Of Documents, And Attendance And Testimony At Deposition, Or, In The Alternative, For Terminating Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 22, 2017, Line 1, DEFENDANT INSIKT, INC. Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Special Interrogatories And Demand For Production Of Documents, And Attendance And Testimony At Deposition, Or, In The Alternative, For Terminating Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Mark LeHocky, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. =(302/JPT/HK)

rulings.Case Title: STEVEN S. MIYAKE VS. ANDREW M. KING ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Discovery Responses And To Have Matters Deemed Admitted.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 22, 2017, Line 4, PLAINTIFF STEVEN MIYAKE Motion To Compel Discovery Responses And To Have Matters Deemed Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Mark LeHocky, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion of plaintiff Steven Miyake for Order (1) Compelling Defendant Andrew King to answer Plaintiff's First Set of Form Interrogatories, First Set of Special Interrogatories, First Requests for Production of Documents, and (2) Deeming Plaintiff's First Requests for Admissions to be Admitted, is granted. Defendant has failed to file any opposition to Plaintiff's discovery motions and good cause appearing, Plaintiff's motion is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to mark@marklehocky.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/HK)

rulings.Case Title: CURTIS GARDNER AND CHRISTINE GARDNER ET AL VS. NISREEN HAROUN-GARDNER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Curtis Gardner And Christine Gardner, And Modern Technology Services, Inc'S Motion To Compel Responses And Further Responses To Discovery And For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions In The Sum Of $19,243.50
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON THURSDAY, JUNE 22, 2017, LINE 8 Plaintiffs Curtis Gardner And Christine Gardner And Modern Technology Services, Inc'S Motion To Compel Responses And Further Responses To Discovery And For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions In The Sum Of $19,243.50 Judge Stephen Murphy will hear this matter at 2:00 p.m. on June 22, 2017 in department 302. Judge Murphy issues the following tentative ruling: OFF CALENDAR as moot. Defendants have served responses. This is without prejudice to plaintiffs' bringing a motion to compel further responses if plaintiffs determine, after meeting and conferring, that the responses are insufficient. Plaintiffs' Motion for Monetary Sanctions of $19,243.50 is DENIED. The Court notes that defendants were in the process of changing counsel and plaintiffs could have obtained an extension to file motions to compel to protect their right to do so. The Court also notes that the amount of sanctions requested by plaintiffs is grossly excessive and far out of proportion to the work required to draft the motions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/SM
